nativeRange.habitat: Tropical and subtropical western-Atlantic reefs, rocky bottoms, calcareous algae beds and adjacent soft sediments; typically hides in crevices, sponges and among rockwork by day and emerges at night to forage.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with caves, ledges and crevices for daytime hiding
a defined territory of rockwork the crab can claim
open sand or rubble foraging areas reached at night
stable, established reef system with calcium, magnesium and iodine within reef-tank norms to support molting
avoid systems with any history of copper-based medication

diet.notes: Opportunistic scavenger and predator. In captivity generally accepts most meaty frozen foods (mysis, brine shrimp, chopped clam, shrimp or fish), sinking pellets and flakes; supplemental targeted feeding is commonly advised to keep the crab away from tankmates. In the wild it has been documented eating bristle worms, small feather-duster worms, tiny molluscs and other small invertebrates, and is widely used as a bristleworm-control invertebrate. Periodic iodine and trace-element supplementation supports successful molting.

goodWith: medium-sized peaceful reef fish that are too large to be ambushed
tangs, larger wrasses and similarly mobile reef fish
established LPS, SPS and most soft corals (with adequate feeding)
robust hermit crabs and snails of similar or larger size in larger tanks
well-fed, well-established reef tanks where bristleworm control is desired
avoidWith: other Arrow Crabs (extremely territorial; will typically fight to the death)
ornamental shrimp such as Coral Banded, Cleaner (Lysmata), Peppermint and Sexy Shrimp
very small or slow-moving fish (e.g. small gobies, dragonets, firefish, seahorses, pipefish)
feather duster worms and other small sessile invertebrates
very small snails and tiny hermit crabs
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: Caribbean reef crab with a triangular carapace, long spider-like legs and a spear-shaped rostrum. Hardy and useful for bristleworm control, but opportunistic predator of ornamental shrimp and small slow fish.
careNotes: Distinctive western-Atlantic majoid crab named for its arrow-shaped body and head, with a triangular carapace, a long spear-like rostrum, very long thin legs (leg span commonly 10-15 cm, sometimes longer) and often blue-violet claw tips on a cream-to-tan body marked with darker lines. Generally regarded as a hardy, beginner-friendly invertebrate to keep alive and is widely sold as a bristleworm-control crab; in captivity it has been documented hunting bristle worms out of crevices using its long legs. The bigger care decision is around tankmates: it is a confirmed opportunistic predator and has been known to attack ornamental shrimp (Coral Banded, cleaner shrimp, peppermint shrimp, sexy shrimp), feather duster worms, very small snails and small slow-moving fish, especially at night. It is therefore best described as 'reef-safe with caution'. Keep only one per tank unless a true bonded pair has been purchased together small; same-sex adults are highly territorial and will typically fight to the death. Like all marine crustaceans it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and any prior copper treatment in the system should be regarded as disqualifying. Drip-acclimate slowly to avoid salinity/pH shock, and add it well after the nitrogen cycle is complete. Note that 'Spider Crab' is sometimes used in the trade for this species, but it is taxonomically distinct from the true cold-water spider crabs of the family Majidae (e.g. Japanese spider crab) - modern taxonomy places S. seticornis in family Inachidae. Source ranges were broadly consistent: temperature 22-28°C (averaged to 22-27°C); specific gravity 1.020-1.026 (averaged to 1.021-1.025); minimum tank size 20-30 US gallons in retailer sources, written here as 100 L (~26 gal) to give the crab a defined territory and reduce risk to tankmates in cramped setups. Designated secondary Reef2Reef Wiki returned HTTP 403; swapped to Aquariumbreeder, Saltwater Aquarium Blog, Fishkeeper and Wikipedia per playbook fallback rule.
breedingNotes: Spawning has been reported occasionally in captivity, with females carrying eggs on the pleopods, but the larvae are planktonic and pass through multiple zoeal stages over a long pelagic period. Larval survival in standard reef setups is extremely low (one source reports around 3-4%); successful rearing generally requires a dedicated planktonic-larvae system with appropriate live foods and is widely regarded as impractical for home aquarists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied LiveAquaria root resolved to this product page via search. Provided baseline husbandry parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), Easy care level, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe with caution, carnivorous diet, family Majidae (corrected to Inachidae from Wikipedia), and explicit warning that it has been known to attack Coral Banded Shrimp and small slow-moving fish.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 and could not be accessed; swapped to other reef-aquarium care guides per playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Detailed husbandry guide: carapace 3-4 cm, leg span up to 15 cm, 24-27°C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.021-1.025, kH 7-12, 20 gal minimum, lifespan up to 5 years, high conspecific aggression, copper-sensitive, family Majoidea/order Decapoda, depth 3-200 m, larval survival 3-4 %.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Confirmed 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.026, dKH 8-12, omnivorous scavenger-predator including bristleworms, semi-aggressive/territorial temperament, reef-safe with risk, breeding very difficult in captivity, color description (cream/yellow body with black, iridescent blue and white stripes).

additional.name: Fishkeeper UK
additional.notes: Cross-checked Caribbean origin, depths up to ~180 m, leg span up to ~20 cm, 22-28°C, SG 1.020-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, nocturnal predator of shrimp/feather dusters/snails and occasionally small fish, copper intolerance, one-per-tank rule.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Stenorhynchus seticornis)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Inachidae, order Decapoda), Western Atlantic distribution (North Carolina/Bermuda through the Caribbean to Brazil/Argentina), shallow sub-tidal habitat, carapace up to 6 cm, nocturnal/territorial behaviour, decoration and cleaning-symbiosis biology.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molts periodically as it grows; adults are commonly reported to molt roughly every three to four weeks, with juveniles molting more often. The crab typically hides in a crevice or under rockwork for 1-2 days around a molt and is extremely vulnerable during this soft-shell window. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium and especially iodine and trace-element levels support successful molts; sudden parameter swings are the most commonly cited cause of failed molts. Shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium and chitin source.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females have been observed carrying eggs on the pleopods in captivity, but larvae are planktonic and pass through a long pelagic phase. Larval survival in standard reef setups is reported around 3-4 % and successful rearing generally requires a dedicated planktonic-larvae system; for home aquarists it is best regarded as not practically breedable.
